Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time during the week, but I'd often find myself distracted or just not feeling it. I usually ended up doing most of my work in bursts when I absolutely had to, like right before something was due. I have about 31 hours a week supposedly dedicated to studying, but a lot of that time I'd be half-focused, scrolling through my phone or just staring at the screen feeling bored.
Q2: It varied massively depending on what was coming up. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be cramming because a deadline was looming. With eight online courses to juggle and limited resources, I couldn't always keep a consistent schedule. Life stuff would get in the way too, and I'd just push studying to whenever I could squeeze it in.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification about something being due. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse or explore. If there wasn't something pressing, I probably wouldn't bother opening the LMS at all. Sometimes a peer would message me asking about an assignment and that would remind me to check in.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never became automatic for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more regularly, especially if we had group work or peer discussions happening. But those moments were temporary. Once the immediate pressure was gone, I'd drift away again until the next thing demanded my attention.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and go through them at my own pace. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in ways that clicked better than the official materials. But honestly, I didn't find most of the LMS stuff that engaging.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the videos most of the time. Watching them felt like a chore, and I'd zone out within minutes. I also didn't bother much with optional readings or extra resources unless they seemed directly relevant to an assignment. When you're bored and stretched thin on resources, you just do the bare minimum to get by.
Q7: If something was clearly going to help me pass or improve my grade, I'd engage with it. Practical stuff that I could apply directly to assignments felt worthwhile. But if it seemed like busy work or filler content, I'd skip it entirely. My time and energy are limited, so I had to be selective about what I actually invested in.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't going to affect my grade, I struggled to find the motivation to do it. Required work at least had consequences, so I'd force myself through it even when I was bored. Optional stuff just felt like extra noise that I didn't have the energy for.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download materials and study from them offline all the time. I'd also use my own notes, look things up on external sites, or work through problems on paper. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me with printed readings or handwritten notes at the kitchen table.
Q10: Plenty of times. I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded PDFs or rewriting notes by hand, and none of that would show up anywhere. Sometimes I'd read through course content once online, then close the LMS and continue working with that material for days. My real study time was way more than what my login data would suggest.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd access something once, download it, and then never go back to the LMS for it. It was easier to work offline, especially since I could study without needing reliable internet the whole time. My resources are limited, so downloading and working offline made more sense for me.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. I'd feel overwhelmed by the workload across all my courses and just mentally check out. The stress would build up, and even though I'm generally resilient, I'd hit walls where I just couldn't bring myself to care about the material anymore.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed about other things in life, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. Competing responsibilities, like work or personal stuff, would take priority. I'd log in less, skim through things faster, and generally do the minimum required to not completely fail.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd be logged in, technically looking at materials, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video and realize I hadn't absorbed a single thing. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually processing the words. Being present on the platform didn't mean I was actually engaged.
Q15: Yeah, I postponed things constantly, especially assignments that seemed tedious or overwhelming. Even when I knew they were important, I'd tell myself I'd do them later. Part of it was boredom, part of it was feeling like I didn't have the mental energy to tackle them properly. Procrastination became a bad habit.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd be pretty inactive until a due date was approaching, then I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS, downloading materials, checking requirements, and rushing to submit. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged with half the content.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but honestly, by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. If feedback was positive, it didn't really change my behavior. If it was critical, I might pay a bit more attention next time, but I often felt too disconnected to really incorporate it meaningfully into my work.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really pull me in. Having eight online courses meant everything blurred together. If the structure was more interactive or demanding of participation, I might have engaged more. But the way things were set up, it was easy to drift along doing the minimum.
Q19: The peer support elements actually helped a bit when I engaged with them. Having discussions with other students made things feel less isolating. But overall, the course design didn't do much to combat my boredom. More interactive or varied content might have kept my attention better instead of just readings and videos.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for things, trying to understand what's expected, or just clicking around without really knowing what to do. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. You can spend hours on a platform without actually accomplishing anything meaningful if you're confused or disengaged.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I didn't log in that much, but I still managed a decent grade because I studied effectively offline. I'd download what I needed, work through it on my own, and show up for the important stuff. The LMS doesn't capture everything that contributes to learning and performance.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They don't know if you were actually thinking about the material or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline work, the handwritten notes, the conversations with peers outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine mental effort into it. It's not just about being present or completing tasks—it's about being invested. For me, real engagement is rare because I'm often just bored and going through the motions to get the grade.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline, the peer conversations I had outside the platform, or the fact that I was genuinely trying despite feeling disconnected most of the time. It also wouldn't capture my boredom or the mental energy it took to push through even when I wasn't interested. There's a whole internal experience that data can't measure.
